name: Pipridae
description: Manakins are small, colorful passerines of Neotropical forests, famous for their elaborate courtship displays at traditional leks. Males often have brilliantly colored plumage and perform complex dance routines.

children.name: Ceratopipra
children.description: Ceratopipra is a genus of small, colorful manakins known for their elaborate courtship dances.

children.name: Ceratopipra mentalis
children.description: The red-capped manakin has a brilliant red crown and black body, found from southern Mexico to Ecuador. Males perform a distinctive side-to-side sliding display on mossy branches.

children.name: Ceratopipra erythrocephala
children.description: The golden-headed manakin has a bright golden-yellow head and black body, widespread across Amazonia and the Guianas. It is one of the most common and well-known manakin species.

children.name: Manacus
children.description: Manacus is a genus of manakins distinguished by their puffed white throat feathers and snapping wing displays.

children.name: Manacus manacus
children.description: The white-bearded manakin is a black-and-white bird with a puffed white throat, found across northern South America. Its lekking display includes rapid leaps between vertical saplings accompanied by mechanical wing snaps.

children.name: Chiroxiphia
children.description: Chiroxiphia is a genus of manakins known for their brilliant blue backs and cooperative courtship displays between males.

children.name: Chiroxiphia pareola
children.description: The blue-backed manakin has a striking sapphire-blue back and red crown, found across Amazonia and the Guianas. Pairs or trios of males perform coordinated leapfrog displays on horizontal branches.

children.name: Pipra
children.description: Pipra is a genus of manakins with elongated tail feathers that form distinctive wire-like extensions.

children.name: Pipra filicauda
children.description: The wire-tailed manakin has bright yellow underparts and two elongated tail feathers that extend beyond the rest of the tail like wires. Males perform acrobatic displays over traditional lek sites.

children.name: Machaeropterus
children.description: Machaeropterus is a genus of manakin with unique modified wing feathers used to produce mechanical sounds during display.

children.name: Machaeropterus deliciosus
children.description: The club-winged manakin has modified secondary wing feathers that it rubs together at high speed to produce a violin-like mechanical trill. It is found in foothill forests of the western Andes.
